title.en: Volume 23: Treatise on Punishment and Law
title.raw: 卷二十三 刑法志 Volume 23: Treatise on Punishment and Law

sentences.zh: 夫人宵天地之䫉，懷五常之性，聰明精粹，有生之最靈者也。

translations.literal: Humans receive the form of Heaven and Earth, bear the nature of the Five Constants, and in clarity and refined essence are the most numinous among living things.
translations.idiomatic: Human beings, bearing the form of Heaven and Earth and the nature of the Five Constants, are the most numinous of living creatures.

sentences.zh: 爪牙不足以供耆欲，趨走不足以避利害，無毛羽以禦寒暑，必將役物以為養，任智而不恃力，此其所以為貴也。

translations.literal: Claws and fangs are not enough to satisfy desires; running is not enough to avoid harm; with no fur or feathers against heat and cold, people must employ things for sustenance, rely on intelligence rather than force; this is why they are precious.
translations.idiomatic: Because humans lack natural weapons and coverings, they survive by organizing the world through intelligence, not brute strength; that is what makes them distinctive.

sentences.zh: 故不仁愛則不能群，不能群則不勝物，不勝物則養不足。

translations.literal: Therefore without benevolence and love one cannot form community; without community one cannot prevail over things; without prevailing over things, nourishment is insufficient.
translations.idiomatic: Without humane solidarity, people cannot form society; without society, they cannot secure resources for life.

sentences.zh: 群而不足，爭心將作，上聖卓然先行敬讓博愛之德者，眾心說而從之。

translations.literal: If in community there is still insufficiency, contentious hearts will arise. The highest sages first practiced reverence, yielding, and broad love; people's hearts delighted and followed them.
translations.idiomatic: When scarcity persists, conflict begins. The sages preempted this by modeling respect, deference, and universal concern, winning willing allegiance.

sentences.zh: 從之成群，是為君矣；

translations.literal: When followers formed into a community, this was called a ruler;
translations.idiomatic: Once people gathered under such leadership, that person became a ruler;

sentences.zh: 歸而往之，是為王矣。

translations.literal: when all turned and went to him, this was called a king.
translations.idiomatic: when all under heaven gravitated to him, he became a true king.

sentences.zh: 洪範曰：「天子作民父母，為天下王。」

translations.literal: The Great Plan says: 'The Son of Heaven acts as father and mother of the people, becoming king for all under heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: As the Great Plan puts it, the Son of Heaven is parent to the people and thus king of the world.

sentences.zh: 聖人取類以正名，而謂君為父母，明仁愛德讓，王道之本也。

translations.literal: Sages took analogies to rectify names and called the ruler father and mother, making clear that benevolence, love, virtue, and yielding are the root of kingly way.
translations.idiomatic: Calling the ruler 'parent' emphasized that humane care and moral deference are the foundation of legitimate kingship.

sentences.zh: 愛待敬而不敗，德須威而久立，故制禮以崇敬，作刑以明威也。

translations.literal: Love must be supported by reverence or it fails; virtue requires authority to endure. Therefore rites were established to exalt reverence and punishments made to clarify authority.
translations.idiomatic: Affection alone is unstable; it must be paired with respect, and virtue must be backed by enforceable authority. Hence both ritual and penal law.

sentences.zh: 聖人既躬明悊之性，必通天地之心，制禮作教，立法設刑，動緣民情，而則天象地。

translations.literal: Since sages personally embodied bright and wise nature, they necessarily communicated with Heaven-and-Earth's intent, making rites and teaching, establishing law and punishment, acting in accord with human feelings and modeling Heaven and Earth.
translations.idiomatic: Because sages understood both human nature and cosmic principle, they built ritual, education, law, and punishment around real human emotions while mirroring cosmic order.

sentences.zh: 故曰先王立禮，「則天之明，因地之性」也。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said former kings established rites by 'patterning Heaven's brightness and following Earth's nature.'
translations.idiomatic: Thus ritual was grounded in celestial clarity and terrestrial pattern.

sentences.zh: 刑罰威獄，以類天之震曜殺戮也；

translations.literal: Penal punishments and prisons correspond to Heaven's thunder, lightning, and killing strokes;
translations.idiomatic: Punitive law mirrors the coercive side of cosmic power-thunder, lightning, and lethal force;

sentences.zh: 溫慈惠和，以效天之生殖長育也。

translations.literal: warmth, kindness, beneficence, and harmony emulate Heaven's generation, growth, and nurture.
translations.idiomatic: while benevolence and care imitate Heaven's life-giving and nurturing functions.

sentences.zh: 《書》云「天秩有禮」，「天討有罪」。

translations.literal: The Documents says, 'Heaven's ordered ranks have rites,' and 'Heaven punishes the guilty.'
translations.idiomatic: As the Documents says, Heaven ordains ritual order and also visits punishment on wrongdoing.

sentences.zh: 故聖人因天秩而制五禮，因天討而作五刑。

translations.literal: Therefore sages, following Heaven's order, made the Five Rites; following Heaven's punishment, made the Five Penalties.
translations.idiomatic: So the sages derived both ritual classification and penal classification from Heaven's own ordering and sanction.

sentences.zh: 大刑用甲兵，其次用斧鉞；

translations.literal: The greatest punishment uses armored troops; next uses axes and halberds;
translations.idiomatic: At the highest level, punishment means military force; next comes execution by weapons;

sentences.zh: 中刑用刀鋸，其次用鑽鑿；

translations.literal: middle punishments use knives and saws; next use boring and chiseling;
translations.idiomatic: middle grades involved mutilating instruments;

sentences.zh: 薄刑用鞭扑。

translations.literal: lighter punishments use whips and rods.
translations.idiomatic: lighter ones used flogging.

sentences.zh: 大者陳諸原野，小者致之市朝，其所繇來者上矣。

translations.literal: Great cases are displayed in fields and wilds; smaller are carried out in markets and courts; their origin is ancient and elevated.
translations.idiomatic: Major penalties were public in open ground, lesser penalties in urban spaces-a very old institutional pattern.

sentences.zh: 自黃帝有涿鹿之戰以定火災，顓頊有共工之陳以定水害。

translations.literal: From Yellow Emperor there was the battle of Zhuolu to settle fire calamities; Zhuanxu had the campaign against Gonggong to settle water disasters.
translations.idiomatic: From the earliest legendary rulers, armed action was used to suppress catastrophic disorder, whether framed as fire or flood threats.

sentences.zh: 唐虞之際，至治之極，猶流共工，放讙兜，竄三苗，殛鯀，然後天下服。

translations.literal: In the era of Yao and Shun, at the extreme of good governance, they still exiled Gonggong, banished Huandou, deported Sanmiao, executed Gun, and only then did the realm submit.
translations.idiomatic: Even under ideal sage rule, severe sanctions remained necessary against major disorder before universal compliance was achieved.

sentences.zh: 夏有甘扈之誓，殷、周以兵定天下矣。

translations.literal: Xia had the oath at Ganshi; Shang and Zhou used arms to settle the world.
translations.idiomatic: Xia, Shang, and Zhou alike relied at key moments on military force to establish order.

sentences.zh: 天下既定，戢臧干戈，教以文德，而猶立司馬之官，設六軍之眾，因井田而制軍賦。

translations.literal: After the realm was settled, they sheathed and stored weapons, taught civil virtue, yet still established the office of Sima and the six armies, and based on well-fields regulated military levy.
translations.idiomatic: Even in peace they promoted civil culture while maintaining a structured military apparatus tied to land and taxation.

sentences.zh: 地方一里為井，井十為通，通十為成，成方十里；

translations.literal: A square li is a well-field; ten wells make a tong; ten tong make a cheng, a square of ten li;
translations.idiomatic: Administrative-military units scaled up from the basic well-field: ten wells to a tong, ten tong to a cheng.

sentences.zh: 成十為終，終十為同，同方百里；

translations.literal: ten cheng make a zhong; ten zhong make a tong, a square of one hundred li;
translations.idiomatic: Further aggregation produced larger territorial blocks up to one-hundred-li squares;

sentences.zh: 同十為封，封十為畿，畿方千里。

translations.literal: ten tong make a feng; ten feng make a ji, a square of one thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: and ultimately to the thousand-li royal domain.

sentences.zh: 有稅有租。

translations.literal: There were both taxes and rents.
translations.idiomatic: The system distinguished tax and rent obligations.

sentences.zh: 稅以足食，賦以足兵。

translations.literal: Taxes sufficed for food; levies sufficed for troops.
translations.idiomatic: Food revenue sustained population; military levy sustained defense.

sentences.zh: 故四井為邑，四邑為丘。

translations.literal: Thus four wells made an yi; four yi made a qiu.
translations.idiomatic: At lower levels, four wells formed a hamlet and four hamlets a larger unit.

sentences.zh: 丘，十六井也，有戎馬一匹，牛三頭。

translations.literal: A qiu is sixteen wells and had one warhorse and three oxen.
translations.idiomatic: Each qiu of sixteen wells carried specified livestock obligations for war support.

sentences.zh: 四丘為甸。

translations.literal: Four qiu make a dian.
translations.idiomatic: Four qiu constituted a dian.

sentences.zh: 甸，六十四井也，有戎馬四匹，兵車一乘，牛十二頭，甲士三人，卒七十二人，干戈備具，是謂乘馬之法。

translations.literal: A dian is sixty-four wells, with four warhorses, one war chariot, twelve oxen, three armored men, and seventy-two foot soldiers, with spears and halberds fully equipped-this is called the law of chariot-and-horse.
translations.idiomatic: A sixty-four-well unit supported a full standardized military package of horses, chariot, draft oxen, officers, infantry, and weapons.

sentences.zh: 一同百里，提封萬井，除山川沈斥，城池邑居，園囿術路，三千六百井，定出賦六千四百井，戎馬四百匹，兵車百乘，此卿大夫采地之大者也，是謂百乘之家。

translations.literal: A tong of one hundred li had ten thousand wells under seal; excluding mountains, waters, marshes, cities, settlements, parks, and roads, 3,600 wells were deducted, leaving 6,400 taxable wells, four hundred warhorses and one hundred chariots-this is the largest allotment of an elite fief, called a 'hundred-chariot house.'
translations.idiomatic: In a hundred-li fief, after non-taxable terrain was removed, 6,400 productive wells supported 400 horses and 100 chariots-the upper scale of grand-official estates.

sentences.zh: 一封三百一十六里，提封十萬井，定出賦六萬四千井，戎馬四千匹，兵車千乘，此諸侯之大者也，是謂千乘之國。

translations.literal: A feng of 316 li had one hundred thousand sealed wells, with 64,000 taxable wells, 4,000 warhorses, and 1,000 chariots-this is the largest feudal state, called a 'thousand-chariot state.'
translations.idiomatic: At major feudal scale, assessed land supported a thousand-chariot military capacity.

sentences.zh: 天子畿方千里，提封百萬井，定出賦六十四萬井，戎馬四萬匹，兵車萬乘，故稱萬乘之主。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven's domain, one thousand li square, had one million sealed wells, 640,000 taxable wells, 40,000 warhorses, and 10,000 chariots; therefore he is called lord of ten thousand chariots.
translations.idiomatic: The royal domain's assessed capacity justified the classic title 'lord of ten thousand chariots.'

sentences.zh: 戎馬車徒干戈素具，春振旅以搜，夏拔舍以苗，秋治兵以獮，冬大閱以狩，皆於農隙以講事焉。

translations.literal: Warhorses, chariots, troops, and weapons being regularly prepared, in spring they assembled for search drill, in summer for seedling drill, in autumn for hunting drill, in winter for grand review-all training done in agricultural slack seasons.
translations.idiomatic: Military readiness was seasonal and synchronized with agricultural downtime, preserving both farming and defense.

sentences.zh: 五國為屬，屬有長；

translations.literal: Five states made a subordinate unit, with a chief;
translations.idiomatic: Five states formed a lower cluster under a chief;

sentences.zh: 十國為連，連有帥；

translations.literal: ten states made a lian, with a commander;
translations.idiomatic: ten formed a larger command under a marshal;

sentences.zh: 三十國為卒，卒有正；

translations.literal: thirty states made a zu, with a proper officer;
translations.idiomatic: thirty formed a corps under a chief officer;

sentences.zh: 二百一十國為州，州有牧。

translations.literal: two hundred ten states made a zhou, with a pastor.
translations.idiomatic: and 210 constituted a regional circuit under a governor.

sentences.zh: 連帥比年簡車，卒正三年簡徒，群牧五載大簡車徒，此先王為國立武足兵之大略也。

translations.literal: Lian commanders annually inspected chariots; corps officers inspected infantry every three years; regional pastors made a grand inspection every five years. This was the broad program by which former kings established military sufficiency.
translations.idiomatic: The old system used regular inspection cycles to maintain force quality without abandoning institutional control.

sentences.zh: 周道衰，法度颦，至齊桓公任用管仲，而國富民安。

translations.literal: When Zhou declined and norms wrinkled, Duke Huan of Qi employed Guan Zhong, and the state became wealthy and the people secure.
translations.idiomatic: As Zhou order eroded, Qi's reform under Guan Zhong restored wealth and stability.

sentences.zh: 公問行伯用師之道，管仲曰：「公欲定卒伍，修甲兵，大國亦將修之，而小國設備，則難以速得志矣。」

translations.literal: The duke asked about hegemony by military means. Guan Zhong said: 'If Your Grace wishes to fix ranks and repair armor and weapons, great states will also repair theirs, and small states will ready defenses, so quick success will be hard.'
translations.idiomatic: Asked how to pursue hegemony, Guan Zhong warned that overt militarization would trigger balancing by both great and small states.

sentences.zh: 於是乃作內政而寓軍令焉，故卒伍定虖里，而軍政成虖郊。

translations.literal: So he made internal governance and lodged military command within it; thus ranks were fixed in the villages and military administration completed in suburbs.
translations.idiomatic: He embedded military organization within civil administration, creating readiness without provoking immediate counter-mobilization.

sentences.zh: 連其什伍，居處同樂，死生同憂，禍福共之，故夜戰則其聲相聞，晝戰則其目相見，緩急足以相死。

translations.literal: He linked tens and fives so they lived and rejoiced together, shared sorrow in life and death, and shared fortune and misfortune. Thus in night battle they heard one another, in day battle saw one another, and in emergencies could die for one another.
translations.idiomatic: By tightly integrating local units into shared daily life, he produced battlefield cohesion and mutual commitment under stress.

sentences.zh: 其教已成，外攘夷狄，內尊天子，以安諸夏。

translations.literal: With this training complete, he repelled barbarians abroad, honored the Son of Heaven within, and stabilized the Huaxia states.
translations.idiomatic: Once internal discipline matured, Qi could defend frontiers, uphold royal legitimacy, and secure the central states.

sentences.zh: 齊威既沒，晉文接之，亦先定其民，作被廬之法，總帥諸侯，迭為盟主。

translations.literal: After Qi's prestige declined, Duke Wen of Jin followed, first settling his people, creating the Bilu system, commanding the lords and alternating as hegemon.
translations.idiomatic: Jin's Duke Wen continued the model: domestic consolidation first, then interstate leadership.

sentences.zh: 然其禮已頗僭差，又隨時苟合以求欲速之功，故不能充王制。

translations.literal: Yet their rites were already somewhat usurping and uneven; they also made expedient compromises for quick gains, so they could not fulfill kingly institutions.
translations.idiomatic: But hegemonic politics remained institutionally compromised and short-term, never achieving true kingly order.

sentences.zh: 二伯之後，寖以陵夷，至魯成公作丘甲，哀公用田賦，搜狩治兵大閱之事皆失其正。

translations.literal: After the two hegemons, decline deepened: by Duke Cheng of Lu creating qiu-armor and Duke Ai using field levy, military hunts and grand reviews all lost correctness.
translations.idiomatic: Post-hegemonic states distorted older military-ritual norms into ad hoc extraction and display.

sentences.zh: 春秋書而譏之，以存王道。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn records and criticizes this, to preserve kingly way.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn Annals condemned these practices to preserve the normative ideal.

sentences.zh: 於是師旅亟動，百姓罷敝，無伏節死難之誼。

translations.literal: Thus armies moved repeatedly and the people were exhausted, lacking loyalty to die for duty and hardship.
translations.idiomatic: Frequent campaigning wore down the populace and eroded sacrificial civic ethos.

sentences.zh: 孔子傷焉，曰：「以不教民戰，是謂棄之。」

translations.literal: Confucius lamented it, saying: 'To make untrained people fight is to abandon them.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius judged untrained conscription as moral abandonment.

sentences.zh: 故稱子路曰：「由也，千乘之國，可使治其賦也。」

translations.literal: So he praised Zilu: 'You, You, in a thousand-chariot state, can be made to govern its levies.'
translations.idiomatic: Hence Confucius' confidence that Zilu could administer military taxation in a major state.

sentences.zh: 而子路亦曰：「千乘之國，攝虖大國之間，加之以師旅，因之以饑饉，由也為之，比及三年，可使有勇，且知方也。」

translations.literal: And Zilu also said: 'In a thousand-chariot state hemmed between great states, pressed by armies and followed by famine, if I governed it, by three years I could make them brave and also know proper direction.'
translations.idiomatic: Zilu claimed that with disciplined administration, even a pressured state could in three years produce both courage and normative order.

sentences.zh: 治其賦兵教以禮誼之謂也。

translations.literal: This means governing levy and troops while teaching them rites and righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Military administration, in this view, must be inseparable from moral-political education.

sentences.zh: 春秋之後，滅弱吞小，並為戰國，稍增講武之禮，以為戲樂，用相夸視。

translations.literal: After the Spring and Autumn, strong destroyed weak and swallowed small, becoming the Warring States; they gradually increased martial display rites as amusements for mutual boasting.
translations.idiomatic: In the Warring States era, military ritual became spectacle and competitive display.

sentences.zh: 而秦更名角抵，先王之禮沒於淫樂中矣。

translations.literal: Qin then renamed it jiaodi; former kings' rites were submerged in licentious entertainment.
translations.idiomatic: By Qin, old ritual forms had been recast as entertainments, their normative substance lost.

sentences.zh: 雄桀之士因勢輔時，作為權詐以相傾覆，吳有孫武，齊有孫臏，魏有吳起，秦有商鞅，皆禽敵立勝，垂著篇籍。

translations.literal: Heroic and ruthless men, taking advantage of the age, made power techniques and deception to overturn one another: Wu had Sun Wu, Qi had Sun Bin, Wei had Wu Qi, Qin had Shang Yang-all captured enemies and secured victories, leaving written works.
translations.idiomatic: Strategists such as Sun Wu, Sun Bin, Wu Qi, and Shang Yang turned military thought toward power and deception, with influential textual legacies.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，合從連衡，轉相攻伐，代為雌雄。

translations.literal: At this time, vertical and horizontal alliances formed; states attacked each other in rotation, taking turns as strong and weak.
translations.idiomatic: Alliance blocs constantly realigned, producing perpetual competitive warfare.

sentences.zh: 齊愍以技擊彊，魏惠以武卒奮，秦昭以銳士勝。

translations.literal: Qi Min relied on technical fighters for strength, Wei Hui on martial graduates for vigor, Qin Zhao on elite troops for victory.
translations.idiomatic: Different states specialized in different military institutions to seek advantage.

sentences.zh: 世方爭於功利，而馳說者以孫、吳為宗。

translations.literal: As the age contended over profit and utility, persuasive theorists took Sun and Wu as their lineage.
translations.idiomatic: With utility dominant, military discourse canonized the Sun-Wu tradition.

sentences.zh: 時唯孫卿明於王道，而非之曰：「彼孫、吳者，上勢利而貴變詐；

translations.literal: Only Xunzi was clear on kingly way and criticized them: 'Those Sun and Wu people esteem advantage and prize changing deception;
translations.idiomatic: Xunzi alone forcefully rejected them, arguing that Sun-Wu methods overvalued expediency and trickery;

sentences.zh: 施於暴亂昏嫚之國，君臣有間，上下離心，政謀不良，故可變而詐也。

translations.literal: Applied in violent, chaotic, and insolent states where ruler and minister are estranged and upper and lower are divided, with bad policy planning, therefore one can rely on change and deception.
translations.idiomatic: such methods work only in already disordered polities fractured by distrust and poor governance.

sentences.zh: 夫仁人在上，為下所卬，猶子弟之衛父兄，若手足之扞頭目，何可當也？

translations.literal: If a benevolent person is above and is looked up to below, as younger kin protect fathers and elder brothers, as hands and feet defend head and eyes-how could he be withstood?
translations.idiomatic: Under genuine benevolent authority, social cohesion resembles familial protection; such a polity is not easily defeated.

sentences.zh: 鄰國望我，歡若親戚，芬若椒蘭，顧視其上，猶焚灼仇讎。

translations.literal: Neighboring states look upon us with joy as kin, fragrant as pepper and orchid; yet when they look at their own rulers, it is as burning enemies.
translations.idiomatic: If we govern well, neighbors are drawn to us warmly, while resenting their own oppressive rulers.

sentences.zh: 人情豈肯為其所惡而攻其所好哉？

translations.literal: Would human feeling ever willingly attack what it likes on behalf of what it hates?
translations.idiomatic: People do not willingly fight for what they detest against what they admire.

sentences.zh: 故以桀攻桀，猶有巧拙；

translations.literal: Therefore when tyrants attack tyrants, there is still skill and clumsiness;
translations.idiomatic: Even tyrant-against-tyrant conflict leaves room for tactical skill;

sentences.zh: 以桀詐堯，若卵投石，夫何幸之有！

translations.literal: but when a tyrant deceives Yao, it is like throwing an egg at stone-how could there be any lucky outcome!
translations.idiomatic: but trying to defeat true sage rule by trickery is like hurling an egg at rock.

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰：『武王載旆，有虔秉鉞，如火烈烈，則莫我敢遏。』

translations.literal: The Odes says: 'King Wu raised his banners, reverently grasped the axe, blazing like fire-none dared block us.'
translations.idiomatic: As the Odes describes King Wu's campaign, moral legitimacy generated unstoppable force.

sentences.zh: 言以仁誼綏民者，無敵於天下也。

translations.literal: This says that one who pacifies people with benevolence and righteousness has no enemy under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Rule grounded in benevolence and justice is strategically unmatched.

sentences.zh: 若齊之技擊，得一首則受賜金。

translations.literal: For Qi's technical fighters, obtaining one head brought a reward in gold.
translations.idiomatic: Qi incentivized shock troops through cash-for-head rewards.

sentences.zh: 事小敵鲧，則媮可用也；

translations.literal: Against small and weak enemies, this may be usable by opportunists;
translations.idiomatic: Such troops may work against minor opponents;

sentences.zh: 事鉅敵堅，則渙然離矣。

translations.literal: against great and firm enemies, they scatter apart.
translations.idiomatic: but against strong adversaries they quickly disintegrate.

sentences.zh: 是亡國之兵也。

translations.literal: This is the army of a doomed state.
translations.idiomatic: That is a military model fit for state collapse.

sentences.zh: 魏氏武卒，衣三屬之甲，操十二石之弩，負矢五十丢，置戈其上，冠冑帶劍，贏三日之糧，日中而趨百里，中試則復其戶，利其田宅。

translations.literal: Wei's martial graduates wore triple-layer armor, handled twelve-stone crossbows, carried fifty arrows, set halberds atop loads, with helmets and swords, carrying three days' grain, and could march one hundred li by noon; if they passed tests, their household obligations were remitted and their fields and houses advantaged.
translations.idiomatic: Wei's elite infantry were heavily equipped and physically tested, then compensated through tax and property privileges.

sentences.zh: 如此，則其地雖廣，其稅必寡，其氣力數年而衰。

translations.literal: Thus even if the territory is broad, taxes must be sparse, and its manpower declines after several years.
translations.idiomatic: Such a force imposes heavy fiscal burdens and erodes demographic strength over time.

sentences.zh: 是危國之兵也。

translations.literal: This is the army of a perilous state.
translations.idiomatic: That too is a dangerous military model.

sentences.zh: 秦人，其生民也骥阨，其使民也酷烈。

translations.literal: As for Qin, its people in life were constrained and its use of the people was harsh and severe.
translations.idiomatic: Qin organized society through strict coercive discipline and severe extraction.

sentences.zh: 劫之以勢，隱之以阨，狃之以賞慶，道之以刑罰，使其民所以要利於上者，非戰無由也。

translations.literal: Coercing them by power, pressing them by hardship, habituating them with rewards and celebrations, guiding them with punishments, making the only way to gain benefit from above be through war.
translations.idiomatic: By combining pressure, reward conditioning, and punishment, Qin made military performance the sole path of advancement.

sentences.zh: 功賞相長，五甲首而隸五家，是最為有數，故能四世有勝於天下。

translations.literal: Merit and reward reinforced each other; five enemy heads bound five households-this was most numerically exact, so for four generations Qin had victory over the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Its quantified merit system produced sustained multi-generational battlefield success.

sentences.zh: 然皆干賞蹈利之兵，庸徒鬻賣之道耳，未有安制矜節之理也。

translations.literal: Yet these were all armies chasing reward and profit, merely a way of common men selling themselves, without principles of settled institutions and restrained norms.
translations.idiomatic: But such forces were fundamentally transactional, lacking stable moral-institutional discipline.

sentences.zh: 故雖地廣兵彊，鰓鰓常恐天下之一合而共軋己也。

translations.literal: Therefore though lands were broad and troops strong, Qin always anxiously feared that if the world united it would crush Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Hence even at peak strength, Qin lived in strategic fear of coalition encirclement.

sentences.zh: 至乎齊桓、晉文之兵，可謂入其域而有節制矣，然猶未本仁義之統也。

translations.literal: As for the armies of Duke Huan of Qi and Duke Wen of Jin, they may be said to have entered their domain with restraint, yet still did not root in the unifying principle of benevolence and righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Huan and Wen improved discipline but still fell short of a fully benevolent-just political foundation.

sentences.zh: 故齊之技擊不可以遇魏之武卒，魏之武卒不可以直秦之銳士，秦之銳士不可以當桓、文之節制，桓、文之節制不可以敵湯、武之仁義。」

translations.literal: Thus Qi's technical fighters cannot meet Wei's martial graduates; Wei's martial graduates cannot directly face Qin's elites; Qin's elites cannot withstand Huan and Wen's disciplined system; Huan and Wen's discipline cannot rival Tang and Wu's benevolence and righteousness.'
translations.idiomatic: Xunzi's hierarchy runs from mercenary tactics up to disciplined hegemony, culminating in morally grounded kingship as the highest military form.

sentences.zh: 故曰：「善師者不陳，善陳者不戰，善戰者不敗，善敗者不亡。」

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: 'One good at commanding does not deploy; one good at deployment does not fight; one good at fighting does not lose; one good at losing does not perish.'
translations.idiomatic: Hence the saying: supreme strategy prevents battle; if battle occurs, it prevents defeat; if defeat comes, it prevents annihilation.

sentences.zh: 若夫舜修百僚，咎繇作士，命以「蠻夷猾夏，寇賊姦軌」，而刑無所用，所謂善師不陳者也。

translations.literal: When Shun ordered the hundred officials and Gao Yao served as judicial officer, tasked with 'barbarians troubling Xia, bandits and rebels,' yet punishment had no use-this is what is meant by one good at commanding not deploying.
translations.idiomatic: Under ideal governance, institutions were so effective that coercive capacity existed but was rarely used.

sentences.zh: 湯、武征伐，陳師誓眾，而放禽桀、紂，所謂善陳不戰者也。

translations.literal: Tang and Wu launched punitive campaigns, deployed armies and swore the troops, yet overthrew and captured Jie and Zhou-this is what is meant by one good at deployment not fighting.
translations.idiomatic: Tang and Wu's legitimacy made victory decisive once forces were arrayed, minimizing prolonged combat.

sentences.zh: 齊桓南服彊楚，使貢周室，北伐山戎，為燕開路，存亡繼絕，功為伯首，所謂善戰不敗者也。

translations.literal: Duke Huan of Qi subdued powerful Chu in the south, made it tribute to Zhou court, campaigned north against mountain Rong and opened routes for Yan, preserving fallen states and continuing severed lines, becoming foremost hegemon-this is what is meant by one good at fighting not losing.
translations.idiomatic: Huan's record shows effective campaigning and interstate stabilization without strategic defeat.

sentences.zh: 楚昭王遭闔廬之禍，國滅出亡，父老送之。

translations.literal: King Zhao of Chu suffered the disaster of Helu; his state was destroyed and he fled in exile, with elders seeing him off.
translations.idiomatic: Even after catastrophic defeat under Wu pressure, King Zhao entered exile with popular support.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「父老反矣！

translations.literal: The king said: 'Elders, return!
translations.idiomatic: The king told them to go back;

sentences.zh: 何患無君？」

translations.literal: Why worry that there will be no ruler?'
translations.idiomatic: he said there was no need to fear lack of a ruler.

sentences.zh: 父老曰：「有君如是其賢也！」

translations.literal: The elders said: 'With a ruler so worthy as this!','
translations.idiomatic: The elders replied that with such a worthy ruler they could not abandon him.

sentences.zh: 相與從之。

translations.literal: and together followed him.
translations.idiomatic: They followed him as a body.

sentences.zh: 或奔走赴秦，號哭請救，秦人憐之謂之出兵。

translations.literal: Some ran to Qin crying and requesting rescue; Qin people pitied them and discussed sending troops.
translations.idiomatic: Others appealed to Qin in tears, and Qin was moved to dispatch aid.

sentences.zh: 二國并力，遂走吳師，昭王返國，所謂善敗不亡者也。

translations.literal: The two states joined force and routed Wu's army; King Zhao returned to his state-this is what is meant by one good at losing not perishing.
translations.idiomatic: With allied support Chu recovered, illustrating how political legitimacy can survive even major military defeat.

sentences.zh: 若秦因四世之勝，據河山之阻，任用白起、王翦豺狼之徒，奮其爪牙，禽獵六國，以并天下。

translations.literal: But Qin, relying on four generations of victories, the barrier of rivers and mountains, and employing men like Bai Qi and Wang Jian as wolfish types, exerted claws and fangs, hunting the six states to unite the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Qin then pursued total conquest through accumulated advantage, strategic geography, and ruthless generals.

sentences.zh: 窮武極詐，士民不附，卒隸之徒，還為敵讎，猋起雲合，果共軋之。

translations.literal: Exhausting martial force and utmost deception, it lacked attachment of soldiers and people; convict and servile troops turned into enemies, rising like whirlwind and clouds, and indeed together crushed Qin.
translations.idiomatic: By overreliance on coercion and deceit, Qin alienated society; its own lower ranks eventually joined the coalition that destroyed it.

sentences.zh: 斯為下矣。

translations.literal: This is the lowest condition.
translations.idiomatic: That is the lowest strategic outcome.

sentences.zh: 凡兵，所以存亡繼絕，救亂除害也。

translations.literal: In general, armies exist to preserve and continue what is on the edge of ruin, to save chaos and remove harm.
translations.idiomatic: The proper function of military force is restorative: preserving order, rescuing the endangered, and eliminating harm.

sentences.zh: 故伊、呂之將，子孫有國，與商周並。

translations.literal: Therefore commanders like Yi Yin and Lü Shang had descendants with states, standing alongside Shang and Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the great founders' strategists left enduring political lineages, their merit recognized alongside dynastic houses.

sentences.zh: 至於末世，苟任詐力，以快貪殘，爭城殺人盈城，爭地殺人滿野。

translations.literal: By late ages, men recklessly relied on deceit and force to satisfy greed and cruelty; contesting for cities they killed until cities were full of dead, contesting for land they killed until fields were covered.
translations.idiomatic: In decadent times, rulers chased gain through fraud and violence, producing mass slaughter in both urban sieges and territorial wars.

sentences.zh: 孫、吳、商、白之徒，皆身誅戮於前，而功滅亡於後。

translations.literal: Followers of Sun, Wu, Shang, and Bai were all themselves executed in front, and their achievements perished afterward.
translations.idiomatic: The lineages of Sunzi, Wuzi, Shang Yang, and Bai Qi ended in violent ruin, and their victories did not endure.

sentences.zh: 報應之勢，各以類至，其道然矣。

translations.literal: The tendency of retribution comes by kind; so the Way naturally works.
translations.idiomatic: Consequences answer in kind; that is how moral causation operates.

sentences.zh: 漢興，高祖躬神武之材，行寬仁之厚，總攬英雄，以誅秦、項。

translations.literal: When Han arose, Gaozu personally possessed numinous martial talent and practiced broad benevolent generosity, gathering heroes to punish Qin and Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: At Han's founding, Gaozu combined military charisma with political magnanimity, uniting talent to defeat Qin and Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 任蕭、曹之文，用良、平之謀，騁陸、酈之辯，明叔孫通之儀，文武相配，大略舉焉。

translations.literal: He employed Xiao and Cao in civil administration, used Liang and Ping in strategy, gave rein to Lu and Li in persuasion, and made clear Shusun Tong's ceremonial system; civil and military matched, and the broad program was established.
translations.idiomatic: By balancing bureaucrats, strategists, diplomats, and ritual specialists, Gaozu aligned civil and military governance into a coherent founding program.

sentences.zh: 天下既定，踵秦而置材官於郡國，京師有南北軍之屯。

translations.literal: After the realm was settled, he followed Qin in placing material officers in commanderies and kingdoms, and in the capital there were garrisons of northern and southern armies.
translations.idiomatic: Once unified, Han retained Qin-style local military offices and permanent capital garrisons.

sentences.zh: 至武帝平百粵，內增七校，外有樓船，皆歲時講肄，修武備云。

translations.literal: By Emperor Wu's pacification of Baiyue, seven internal regiments were added and outside there were towered warships; all were drilled yearly and seasonally, maintaining martial preparedness.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperor Wu's southern campaigns, Han expanded both land and naval forces and institutionalized regular training.

sentences.zh: 至元帝時，以貢禹議，始罷角抵，而未正治兵振旅之事也。

translations.literal: By Emperor Yuan's time, on Gong Yu's advice, jiaodi was first abolished, though military training and inspections were not yet properly restored.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Yuan, jiaodi spectacle was removed, but proper military-ritual discipline was still not fully reestablished.

sentences.zh: 古人有言：「天生五材，民並用之，廢一不可，誰能去兵？」

translations.literal: An old saying says: 'Heaven produced five materials, and people use all of them; none can be discarded-who can remove arms?'
translations.idiomatic: As the old maxim holds, force cannot be abolished from human society.

sentences.zh: 鞭扑不可弛於家，刑罰不可廢於國，征伐不可偃於天下；

translations.literal: Whips cannot be relaxed in households, punishments cannot be abolished in states, and campaigns cannot be put away in the world;
translations.idiomatic: Domestic discipline, state law, and military force each remain necessary in their domains;

sentences.zh: 用之有本末，行之有逆順耳。

translations.literal: it is only that their use has root and branch, and their execution has proper and improper order.
translations.idiomatic: the issue is sequence and measure, not absolute presence or absence.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「工欲善其事，必先利其器。」

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'If a craftsman wishes to do good work, he must first sharpen his tools.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius taught that effective action requires proper instruments first.

sentences.zh: 文德者，帝王之利器；

translations.literal: Civil virtue is the sharp instrument of emperors and kings;
translations.idiomatic: For kingship, civil virtue is the primary instrument;

sentences.zh: 威武者，文德之輔助也。

translations.literal: martial authority is an assistant to civil virtue.
translations.idiomatic: military force serves as its supporting arm.

sentences.zh: 夫文之所加者深，則武之所服者大；

translations.literal: Where civil influence extends deeply, what martial force can subdue becomes great;
translations.idiomatic: The deeper moral influence runs, the broader military submission becomes;

sentences.zh: 德之所施者博，則威之所制者廣。

translations.literal: where virtue is bestowed broadly, what authority can control is wide.
translations.idiomatic: the wider virtue is applied, the wider coercive order can be stabilized.

sentences.zh: 三代之盛，至於刑錯兵寢者，其本末有序，帝王之極功也。

translations.literal: In the flourishing Three Dynasties, when punishments were set aside and arms rested, root and branch were in order-this was the utmost achievement of emperors and kings.
translations.idiomatic: The Three Dynasties reached penal and military minimalism only after correctly ordering moral foundations and coercive means.

sentences.zh: 昔周之法，建三典以刑邦國，詰四方：一曰，刑新邦用輕典；

translations.literal: In former Zhou law, three codes were established to punish states and interrogate the four quarters: first, in punishing a new state use the light code;
translations.idiomatic: Zhou's tripartite penal doctrine prescribed lighter law for newly integrated polities;

sentences.zh: 二曰，刑平邦用中典；

translations.literal: second, in punishing a balanced state use the middle code;
translations.idiomatic: medium law for stable polities;

sentences.zh: 三曰，刑亂邦用重典。

translations.literal: third, in punishing a disordered state use the heavy code.
translations.idiomatic: and heavy law for chaotic ones.

sentences.zh: 五刑，墨罪五百，劓罪五百，宮罪五百，刖罪五百，殺罪五百，所謂刑平邦用中典者也。

translations.literal: Under the Five Penalties: five hundred ink-face crimes, five hundred nose-cutting crimes, five hundred castration crimes, five hundred foot-cutting crimes, and five hundred capital crimes-this is what is called using the middle code for a balanced state.
translations.idiomatic: The so-called medium code enumerated graded corporal and capital penalties in standardized counts.

sentences.zh: 凡殺人者踣諸市，墨者使守門，劓者使守關，宮者使守內，刖者使守囿，完者使守積。

translations.literal: Generally, killers were cast down in market execution; tattooed criminals guarded gates; nose-cut criminals guarded passes; castrated guarded inner quarters; foot-cut guarded parks; and completed-term convicts guarded granaries.
translations.idiomatic: Penalty types were tied to assigned labor posts, from gate and pass duties to granary and park custody.

sentences.zh: 其奴，男子入于罪隸，女子入舂槁。

translations.literal: As for their slaves, men entered penal servitude, women entered pounding and chaffing labor.
translations.idiomatic: Male convicts became penal bond-laborers; females were assigned grain-processing labor.

sentences.zh: 凡有爵者，與七十者，與未齔者，皆不為奴。

translations.literal: Any with rank, and those seventy or older, and those not yet with adult teeth, were all not made slaves.
translations.idiomatic: Rank holders, elders, and very young minors were exempted from enslavement.

sentences.zh: 周道既衰，穆王眊荒，命甫侯度時作刑，以詰四方。

translations.literal: After Zhou declined, King Mu became dim and lax, ordering Marquis Fu to measure the times and make punishments to question the four quarters.
translations.idiomatic: As Zhou weakened, punitive legislation was expanded under King Mu through the code associated with Marquis Fu.

sentences.zh: 墨罰之屬千，劓罰之屬千，髕罰之屬五百，宮罰之屬三百，大辟之罰其屬二百。

translations.literal: The categories became: one thousand for ink penalties, one thousand for nose penalties, five hundred for kneecap penalties, three hundred for castration penalties, and two hundred categories under death penalty.
translations.idiomatic: The punitive taxonomy under this later code was far larger and harsher than earlier norms.

sentences.zh: 五刑之屬三千，蓋多於平邦中典五百章，所謂刑亂邦用重典者也。

translations.literal: The categories of Five Penalties were three thousand, greater than the five hundred articles of the balanced-state middle code-this is called using heavy code for a disordered state.
translations.idiomatic: This three-thousand-item structure exemplified heavy-law governance for disorder.

sentences.zh: 春秋之時，王道寖壞，教化不行，子產相鄭而鑄刑書。

translations.literal: By Spring and Autumn times, kingly way was increasingly ruined and teaching did not function; Zichan governed Zheng and cast penal statutes in bronze.
translations.idiomatic: As royal norms collapsed, Zheng's Zichan famously codified criminal law in public bronze text.

sentences.zh: 晉叔嚮非之曰：「昔先王議事以制，不為刑辟。

translations.literal: Jin's Shuxiang criticized it: 'Former kings deliberated affairs and regulated, and did not make penal statutes public.
translations.idiomatic: Shuxiang objected that earlier kings ruled through deliberative norms rather than public penal codification.

sentences.zh: 懼民之有爭心也，猶不可禁禦，是故閑之以誼，糾之以政，行之以禮，守之以信，奉之以仁；

translations.literal: Fearing people would have contentious hearts-and even then they were hard to restrain-so they constrained by righteousness, corrected by policy, practiced by rites, kept by trust, and supported by benevolence;
translations.idiomatic: To prevent litigious contention, governance had relied on layered moral-political discipline: righteousness, policy, rite, trust, and benevolence;

sentences.zh: 制為祿位以勸其從，嚴斷刑罰以威其淫。

translations.literal: they established rank and office rewards to encourage compliance, and made punishments severe and decisive to overawe licentiousness.
translations.idiomatic: institutional incentives and decisive penalties worked together under a moral framework.

sentences.zh: 懼其未也，故誨之以忠，霭之以行，教之以務，使之以和，臨之以敬，蒞之以彊，斷之以剛。

translations.literal: Still fearing insufficiency, they taught loyalty, covered with conduct, instructed duties, employed harmony, approached with reverence, governed with strength, and judged with firmness.
translations.idiomatic: Even so they reinforced order through constant ethical training, administrative harmony, and firm adjudication.

sentences.zh: 猶求聖哲之上，明察之官，忠信之長，慈惠之師。

translations.literal: They still sought superior sages, perceptive officials, trustworthy leaders, and benevolent teachers.
translations.idiomatic: They prioritized selecting high-caliber moral and administrative personnel.

sentences.zh: 民於是乎可任使也，而不生禍亂。

translations.literal: Only then could the people be employed without generating disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Under such conditions, the populace could be mobilized without social breakdown.

sentences.zh: 民知有辟，則不忌於上，並有爭心，以徵於書，而徼幸以成之，弗可為矣。

translations.literal: If people know fixed penal texts, they cease fearing superiors, all develop contentious hearts, and by citing texts seek lucky evasion-so governance becomes impossible.
translations.idiomatic: Public legalism alone, he argued, breeds technical litigation and opportunistic evasion.

sentences.zh: 夏有亂政而作禹刑，商有亂政而作湯刑，周有亂政而作九刑。

translations.literal: Xia had disordered governance and so made Yu penalties; Shang had disordered governance and made Tang penalties; Zhou had disordered governance and made Nine Penalties.
translations.idiomatic: Major penal codifications historically arose in periods of political disorder.

sentences.zh: 三辟之興，皆叔世也。

translations.literal: The rise of these three penal systems all belonged to declining ages.
translations.idiomatic: Thus codified penal expansion marks late-decline politics, not high-order rule.

sentences.zh: 今吾子相鄭國，制參辟，鑄刑書，將以靖民，不亦難乎！

translations.literal: Now you govern Zheng and make a mixed penal code, casting penal text, wishing to pacify people-is that not difficult!
translations.idiomatic: Shuxiang warned Zichan that legal codification would not easily produce social pacification.

sentences.zh: 《詩》曰：『儀式刑文王之德，日靖四方。』

translations.literal: The Odes says: 'Modeling King Wen's virtue, daily pacifying the four quarters.'
translations.idiomatic: As the Odes says, order comes by emulating King Wen's virtue.

sentences.zh: 又曰：『儀刑文王，萬邦作孚。』

translations.literal: It also says: 'Taking King Wen as model, the myriad states become trusting.'
translations.idiomatic: Likewise, moral modeling creates political trust across states.

sentences.zh: 如是，何辟之有？

translations.literal: If so, what need is there for penal statutes?
translations.idiomatic: If virtue governs, why foreground penal text?

sentences.zh: 民知爭端矣，將棄禮而徵於書。

translations.literal: Once people know dispute endpoints, they will abandon rites and litigate by text.
translations.idiomatic: Litigation culture will replace ritual culture.

sentences.zh: 錐刀之末，將盡爭之，亂獄滋豐，貨賂並行。

translations.literal: Even down to awl-and-knife trifles they will contest everything; disorderly lawsuits multiply and bribery runs together.
translations.idiomatic: Petty technical disputes will explode, along with corruption in adjudication.

sentences.zh: 終子之世，鄭其敗虖！」

translations.literal: By your generation's end, Zheng will likely fail!'
translations.idiomatic: He predicted Zheng's eventual weakening as a result.

sentences.zh: 子產報曰：「若吾子之言，僑不材，不能及子孫，吾以救世也。」

translations.literal: Zichan replied: 'If as you say-I, Qiao, am unworthy and cannot reach descendants-I still do this to save the present age.'
translations.idiomatic: Zichan answered pragmatically that immediate rescue of his own age outweighed long-term ideal concerns.

sentences.zh: 媮薄之政，自是滋矣。

translations.literal: From this, opportunistic and thin governance increasingly grew.
translations.idiomatic: Thereafter instrumental and morally thinned governance intensified.

sentences.zh: 孔子傷之，曰：「導之以德，齊之以禮，有恥且格；

translations.literal: Confucius lamented it, saying: 'Guide with virtue and align with rites, and people will have shame and also rectify;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius contrasted moral-ritual governance, which cultivates internal shame and self-correction,

sentences.zh: 導之以政，齊之以刑，民免而無恥。」

translations.literal: guide with policy and align with punishments, and people avoid penalties but have no shame.'
translations.idiomatic: with coercive governance, which produces compliance without moral transformation.

sentences.zh: 「禮樂不興，則刑罰不中；

translations.literal: 'If rites and music do not flourish, punishments will not hit the mark;
translations.idiomatic: Without ritual-music order, penal judgment loses calibration;

sentences.zh: 刑罰不中，則民無所錯手足。」

translations.literal: if punishments do not hit the mark, the people will have nowhere to place hands and feet.'
translations.idiomatic: miscalibrated punishment leaves society disoriented and insecure.

sentences.zh: 孟氏使陽膚為士師，問於曾子，亦曰：「上失其道，民散久矣。

translations.literal: When the Meng clan made Yang Fu judicial officer, he asked Zengzi, who likewise said: 'When superiors lose the Way, people have long been scattered.
translations.idiomatic: Zengzi likewise linked legal disorder to prior collapse of moral leadership.

sentences.zh: 如得其情，則哀矜而勿喜。」

translations.literal: If one obtains the facts, then be sorrowful and compassionate, and do not rejoice.'
translations.idiomatic: Even in successful prosecution, judges should respond with compassion, not triumph.

sentences.zh: 陵夷至於戰國，韓任申子，秦用商鞅，連相坐之法，造參夷之誅；

translations.literal: Declining down to the Warring States, Han employed Shenzi, Qin used Shang Yang: laws of mutual implication and punishments of annihilating collateral lines were created;
translations.idiomatic: By Warring States times, collective-liability and extreme punitive innovations became standard in some states.

sentences.zh: 增加肉刑、大辟，有鑿顛、抽脅、鑊亨之刑。

translations.literal: corporal and capital penalties were increased, including skull-drilling, rib-pulling, and cauldron-boiling punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Severe torture and execution forms proliferated.

sentences.zh: 至於秦始皇，兼吞戰國，遂毀先王之法，滅禮誼之官，專任刑罰，躬操文墨，晝斷獄，夜理書，自程決事，日縣石之一。

translations.literal: By Qin Shihuang, after swallowing the Warring States, he destroyed former kings' laws, eliminated offices of rites and righteousness, exclusively used punishments, personally handled written cases-day adjudicating prisons, night managing documents, deciding matters by fixed quotas, one shi of records per day.
translations.idiomatic: After unification, Qin centralized legal bureaucracy under the emperor, suppressing ritual institutions and ruling through document-intensive penal administration.

sentences.zh: 而姦邪並生，赭衣塞路，囹圄成市，天下愁怨，潰而叛之。

translations.literal: Then wickedness arose together, red-clothed convicts filled roads, prisons became marketplaces, the world grieved and resented, collapsed and rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Hyper-penal governance produced mass criminalization, prison overexpansion, and ultimately revolt.

sentences.zh: 漢興，高祖初入關，約法三章曰：「殺人者死，傷人及盜抵罪。」

translations.literal: When Han arose, Gaozu first entering the passes made the Three Articles: 'Killer dies; injuring others and theft are punished according to crime.'
translations.idiomatic: At conquest, Gaozu's concise Three Articles sharply reduced Qin legal complexity.

sentences.zh: 蠲削煩苛，兆民大說。

translations.literal: He remitted and cut burdensome harshness, and the myriad people greatly rejoiced.
translations.idiomatic: Abolition of oppressive clauses won broad popular support.

sentences.zh: 其後四夷未附，兵革未息，三章之法不足以禦姦，於是相國蕭何雳摭秦法，取其宜於時者，作律九章。

translations.literal: Later, with frontier peoples not yet attached and warfare not yet ceased, the Three Articles were insufficient against crime; so Chancellor Xiao He selected from Qin law those fitting the time and made Nine-Chapter statutes.
translations.idiomatic: As security pressures rose, Han expanded from minimal law to Xiao He's Nine-Chapter code.

sentences.zh: 當孝惠、高后時，百姓新免毒酿，人欲長幼養老。

translations.literal: In Emperor Hui and Empress Gao's time, people had only newly escaped toxic cruelty and desired to raise young and support elders.
translations.idiomatic: Early Han society sought recovery and household stability after Qin-era trauma.

sentences.zh: 蕭、曹為相，填以無為，從民之欲，而不擾亂，是以衣食滋殖，刑罰用稀。

translations.literal: Xiao and Cao were chancellors, filling governance with nonaction, following people's desires without disturbance, so clothing and food multiplied and punishments were rarely used.
translations.idiomatic: Under Huang-Lao style minimal interference, economic recovery accelerated and penal severity declined.

sentences.zh: 丞相張蒼、御史大夫馮敬奏言：「肉刑所以禁姦，所由來者久矣。

translations.literal: Chancellor Zhang Cang and Censor-in-chief Feng Jing memorialized: 'Corporal penalties forbidding wickedness have existed long.
translations.idiomatic: Later ministers argued that corporal punishment had deep institutional precedent.

sentences.zh: 陛下下明詔，憐萬民之一有過被刑者終身不息，及罪人欲改行為善而道亡繇至，於盛德，臣等所不及也。

translations.literal: Your Majesty's bright edict pities that when one person of ten thousand is punished, suffering never ends for life, and that guilty persons wishing to reform and do good have no path to reach it-this is virtue beyond us ministers.
translations.idiomatic: They acknowledged imperial concern that lifelong corporal penalties blocked moral rehabilitation.

sentences.zh: 臣謹議請定律曰：諸當完者，完為城旦舂；

translations.literal: Your servants respectfully propose fixing statutes: those who should be castrated instead become wall-building and pounding labor convicts;
translations.idiomatic: They proposed commutation schedules replacing some mutilations with hard labor terms;

sentences.zh: 當黥者，髡鉗為城旦舂；

translations.literal: those who should be tattooed instead are shaven and shackled into wall-building and pounding labor;
translations.idiomatic: tattoo penalties were converted to shaved-and-shackled labor;

sentences.zh: 當劓者，笞三百；

translations.literal: those who should have noses cut are whipped three hundred;
translations.idiomatic: nose-cutting became 300 lashes;

sentences.zh: 當斬左止者，笞五百；

translations.literal: those who should have left foot cut are whipped five hundred;
translations.idiomatic: left-foot amputation became 500 lashes;

sentences.zh: 當斬右止，及殺人先自告，及吏坐受賕枉法，守縣官財物而即盜之，已論命復有笞罪者，皆棄市。

translations.literal: those who should have right foot cut, and killers who first self-report, and officials guilty of accepting bribes and bending law, and those guarding state property yet stealing it, and those already sentenced to death plus flogging crimes-all are executed in the market.
translations.idiomatic: A range of aggravated and official-corruption offenses remained capital, with public marketplace execution.

sentences.zh: 罪人獄已決，完為城旦舂，滿三歲為鬼薪白粲。

translations.literal: After verdict, those commuted to wall-building labor serve three years then become ghost-fuel and white-grain laborers.
translations.idiomatic: Post-sentence labor moved through staged penal-service categories.

sentences.zh: 鬼薪白粲一歲，為隸臣妾。

translations.literal: After one year as ghost-fuel/white-grain laborer, they become bonded male or female servants.
translations.idiomatic: One service stage transitioned convicts into state bond-servitude.

sentences.zh: 隸臣妾一歲，免為庶人。

translations.literal: After one year as bonded servant, they are released as commoners.
translations.idiomatic: Completion of term could restore commoner status.

sentences.zh: 隸臣妾滿二歲，為司寇。

translations.literal: After two full years as bonded servant, they become judicial laborers.
translations.idiomatic: Extended service could shift them into judicial labor classification.

sentences.zh: 司寇一歲，及作如司寇二歲，皆免為庶人。

translations.literal: After one year as judicial laborer, and likewise those working as judicial labor for two years, all are released as commoners.
translations.idiomatic: Further service terms eventually led to formal release.

sentences.zh: 其亡逃及有罪耐以上，不用此令。

translations.literal: Those who flee or whose crimes are at or above exile-resistance grades do not use this order.
translations.idiomatic: Escapers and higher-grade offenders were excluded from commutation rules.

sentences.zh: 前令之刑城旦舂歲而非禁錮者，如完為城旦舂歲數以免。

translations.literal: For earlier orders where wall-building labor terms were non-incarcerative, years are counted as with the commuted wall-building terms for release.
translations.idiomatic: Legacy penal terms were harmonized with the new commutation-release schedule.

sentences.zh: 臣昧死請。」

translations.literal: Your servants beg at risk of death.
translations.idiomatic: The ministers closed with the standard death-risk petition formula.

sentences.zh: 制曰：「可。」

translations.literal: The edict said: 'Approved.'
translations.idiomatic: The throne approved the proposal.

sentences.zh: 是後，外有輕刑之名，內實殺人。

translations.literal: After this, externally there was the name of lighter penalties, but internally in fact it killed people.
translations.idiomatic: In practice, nominal commutation often produced lethal outcomes.

sentences.zh: 斬右止者又當死。

translations.literal: Those with right-foot amputation sentence still should die.
translations.idiomatic: Some commuted categories remained effectively capital.

sentences.zh: 斬左止者笞五百，當劓者笞三百，率多死。

translations.literal: Those for left-foot amputation whipped five hundred, and those for nose penalty whipped three hundred-mostly died.
translations.idiomatic: Extreme flogging rates caused high mortality.

sentences.zh: 及至孝武即位，外事四夷之功，內盛耳目之好，徵發煩數，百姓貧耗，窮民犯法，酷吏擊斷，姦軌不勝。

translations.literal: By Emperor Wu's accession, externally he pursued achievements against frontier peoples, internally indulged sensory tastes; levies and conscriptions were frequent, people impoverished and depleted; poor people broke law, harsh officials struck and condemned, and wrongdoing could not be exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wu, military expansion and fiscal extraction intensified social strain, while harsh enforcement failed to control rising crime.

sentences.zh: 於是招進張湯、趙禹之屬，條定法令，作見知故縱、監臨部主之法，緩深故之罪，急縱出之誅。

translations.literal: Then Zhang Tang, Zhao Yu and their type were recruited, sorting and fixing laws and ordinances, making statutes on 'seeing and knowing, intentional release,' and 'supervisory department heads,' relaxing deep-old crimes yet sharpening punishment for deliberate release.
translations.idiomatic: Legal technocrats expanded doctrinal categories and tightened accountability frameworks, especially around supervisory responsibility.

sentences.zh: 其後姦猾巧法，轉相比況，禁罔寖密。

translations.literal: Afterward, crafty offenders manipulated law, analogizing case to case; prohibitive nets grew increasingly dense.
translations.idiomatic: Both officials and offenders exploited technical legal analogies, producing ever denser legal restrictions.

sentences.zh: 律令凡三百五十九章，大辟四百九條，千八百八十二事，死罪決事比萬三千四百七十二事。

translations.literal: Statutes and ordinances totaled 359 chapters; capital clauses 409; 1,882 matters; and precedent analogies deciding death cases 13,472 matters.
translations.idiomatic: Han law became massively complex in chapters, clauses, and precedent files.

sentences.zh: 文書盈於几閣，典者不能遍睹。

translations.literal: Documents filled desks and shelves; custodians could not view them all.
translations.idiomatic: The legal archive grew beyond practical administrative mastery.

sentences.zh: 是以郡國承用者駮，或罪同而論異。

translations.literal: Thus commanderies and kingdoms used them inconsistently; sometimes same crime received different judgments.
translations.idiomatic: Uneven implementation generated sentencing inconsistency across jurisdictions.

sentences.zh: 姦吏因緣為市，所欲活則傅生議，所欲陷則予死比，議者咸冤傷之。

translations.literal: Corrupt officials used this as market leverage: those they wished to save got arguments for life, those they wished to trap got death precedents; discussants all lamented this injustice.
translations.idiomatic: Legal complexity enabled prosecutorial rent-seeking, with life/death outcomes manipulated through selective precedent.

sentences.zh: 宣帝自在閭閻而知其若此，及即尊位，廷史路溫舒上疏，言秦有十失，其一尚存，治獄之吏是也。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan, from commoner life, knew it was so; after taking the throne, court historian Lu Wenshu memorialized that Qin had ten failures, one still remaining: officials who manage prisons.
translations.idiomatic: Because Emperor Xuan knew grassroots legal abuse firsthand, memorial criticism of judicial bureaucracy resonated with him.

sentences.zh: 語在溫舒傳。

translations.literal: The words are in Wenshu's biography.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial text is preserved in Lu Wenshu's biography.

sentences.zh: 上深愍焉，乃下詔曰：「間者吏用法，巧文寖深，是朕之不德也。

translations.literal: The emperor deeply pitied this, and issued edict: 'Recently officials using law have grown deeply into clever wording-this is my lack of virtue.
translations.idiomatic: He issued an edict blaming over-technical legalism on failures of imperial governance.

sentences.zh: 夫決獄不當，使有罪興邪，不辜蒙戮，父子悲恨，朕甚傷之。

translations.literal: If judgments are improper, the guilty rise in wickedness and innocents suffer execution; fathers and sons grieve with hatred-I am deeply pained by this.
translations.idiomatic: Misjudged cases both embolden true criminals and destroy innocents, causing profound social trauma.

sentences.zh: 今遣廷史與郡鞠獄，任輕祿薄，其為置廷平，秩六百石，員四人。

translations.literal: Now dispatch court historians to join commandery case-hearings; their rank is light and salary thin, so establish Court Equalizers, rank six-hundred shi, four positions.
translations.idiomatic: He created four 'Court Equalizer' posts to participate in local adjudication and improve balance.

sentences.zh: 其務平之，以稱朕意。」

translations.literal: Their task is to level judgments, so as to accord with my intent.'
translations.idiomatic: Their mandate was to standardize and humanize sentencing in line with imperial policy.

sentences.zh: 於是選于定國為廷尉，求明察寬恕黃霸等以為廷平，季秋後請讞。

translations.literal: Accordingly Yu Dingguo was selected as chief justice, and bright, observant, lenient men like Huang Ba sought as Court Equalizers; after late autumn, review memorials were requested.
translations.idiomatic: He appointed capable judicial leadership and staffed equalizer roles with officials known for careful and lenient judgment.

sentences.zh: 時上常幸宣室，齋居而決事，獄刑號為平矣。

translations.literal: At that time the emperor often went to Xuanshi Hall, residing in fasting while deciding affairs, and prison judgments were called level.
translations.idiomatic: With direct imperial attention to adjudication, penal practice was for a time regarded as comparatively fair.

sentences.zh: 時涿郡太守鄭昌上疏言：「聖王置諫爭之臣者，非以崇德，防逸豫之生也；

translations.literal: At that time Zhuojun governor Zheng Chang memorialized: 'Sage kings establish remonstrating ministers not to ornament virtue, but to prevent the rise of indulgent ease;
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Chang argued that remonstrance institutions exist as safeguards against elite drift, not mere moral display;

sentences.zh: 立法明刑者，非以為治，救衰亂之起也。

translations.literal: and establishing law and clear punishments is not for governance itself, but to rescue the rise of decline and disorder.
translations.idiomatic: law is remedial for disorder, not the highest form of rule.

sentences.zh: 今明主躬垂明聽，雖不置廷平，獄將自正；

translations.literal: Now the bright ruler personally hangs clear hearing; even without Court Equalizers, judgments would self-correct;
translations.idiomatic: Given active imperial oversight, he argued, adjudication could already be corrected;

sentences.zh: 若開後嗣，不若刪定律令。

translations.literal: as opening to later heirs, better than this is to revise and fix statutes and ordinances.
translations.idiomatic: for long-term governance, codification reform was more important than temporary offices.

sentences.zh: 律令一定，愚民知所避，姦吏無所弄矣。

translations.literal: Once statutes and ordinances are fixed, foolish people know what to avoid, and corrupt officials have nothing to manipulate.
translations.idiomatic: A clear, stable code would both guide commoners and reduce discretionary abuse by officials.

sentences.zh: 今不正其本，而置廷平以理其末也，政衰聽怠，則廷平將招權而為亂首矣。」

translations.literal: Now if one does not correct the root but sets Court Equalizers to manage the branches, when governance declines and hearings grow lax, the Court Equalizers will gather power and become the head of disorder.
translations.idiomatic: If fundamentals are left untouched and only procedural offices are added, those offices can eventually become new centers of abuse.

sentences.zh: 宣帝未及修正。

translations.literal: Emperor Xuan did not have time to correct it.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xuan never fully carried out structural reform.

sentences.zh: 至元帝初立，乃下詔曰：「夫法令者，所以抑暴扶弱，欲其難犯而易避也。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan first ascended, he issued an edict: 'Laws and ordinances are meant to restrain the violent and support the weak, so they should be hard to violate and easy to avoid.'
translations.idiomatic: At the start of Emperor Yuan's reign, an edict reaffirmed that law should protect the weak and remain comprehensible.

sentences.zh: 今律令煩多而不約，自典文者不能分明，而欲羅元元之不逮，斯豈刑中之意哉！

translations.literal: 'Now statutes are numerous and not concise; even legal specialists cannot distinguish them clearly, yet we would net the common people for what they cannot reach-is this the meaning of proper punishment?'
translations.idiomatic: The edict criticized legal overcomplexity that trapped ordinary people even when experts struggled to interpret it.

sentences.zh: 其議律令可蠲除輕減者，條奏，唯在便安萬姓而已。」

translations.literal: 'Deliberate what can be remitted, removed, or reduced; submit by categories, with only the convenience and safety of the myriad people in view.'
translations.idiomatic: Officials were ordered to propose simplifications and reductions oriented to public welfare.

sentences.zh: 至成帝河平中，復下詔曰：「甫刑云『五刑之屬三千，大辟之罰其屬二百』，今大辟之刑千有餘條，律令煩多，百有餘萬言，奇請它比，日以益滋，自明習者不知所由，欲以曉喻眾庶，不亦難乎！

translations.literal: In Chengdi's Heping years another edict said: 'The Fuxing says the Five Penalties have three thousand categories and capital punishment two hundred; now capital clauses are over a thousand, statutes and ordinances are cumbersome and many, over a million words, extraordinary petitions and analogies increase daily; even specialists do not know the basis-how can we explain this to the masses?'
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Cheng, a further reform edict lamented explosive growth in legal text and death-penalty clauses, making the system unintelligible.

sentences.zh: 於以羅元元之民，夭絕亡辜，豈不哀哉！

translations.literal: 'If by this we net the common people, cutting off lives of innocents, is it not tragic!'
translations.idiomatic: The court explicitly linked legal complexity to wrongful death.

sentences.zh: 其與中二千石、二千石、博士及明習律令者議減死刑及可蠲除約省者，令較然易知，條奏。

translations.literal: 'Have officials of 2,000-shi rank, erzhongqianshi, scholars, and legal experts deliberate reductions of death penalties and what can be removed and simplified, making them clear and easy to know, and submit by categories.'
translations.idiomatic: A broad review group was convened to reduce capital punishment and simplify statutes.

sentences.zh: 書不云乎？

translations.literal: 'Does not the Documents say?
translations.idiomatic: The edict cited scriptural authority:

sentences.zh: 『惟刑之恤哉！』

translations.literal: 'Take care with punishments!'
translations.idiomatic: 'Be cautious in punishment.'

sentences.zh: 其審核之，務準古法，朕將盡心覽焉。」

translations.literal: 'Examine and verify carefully, striving to align with ancient law; I will personally review with full attention.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor promised personal scrutiny and alignment with classical standards.

sentences.zh: 有司無仲山父將明之材，不能因時廣宣主恩，建立明制，為一代之法，而徒鉤摭微細，毛舉數事，以塞詔而已。

translations.literal: But officials lacked the talent of Zhongshan Fu to clarify matters, and could not broadenly proclaim the ruler's grace according to the times, establish clear institutions, and make law for an age; they only picked tiny details and raised a few items to block the edict.
translations.idiomatic: Because officials lacked high-level statecraft, they answered reform edicts with piecemeal technicalities instead of systemic restructuring.

sentences.zh: 是以大議不立，遂以至今。

translations.literal: Therefore major deliberation was not established, and so it has remained to this day.
translations.idiomatic: As a result, major legal reform repeatedly stalled.

sentences.zh: 議者或曰，法難數變，此庸人不達，疑塞治道，聖智之所常患者也。

translations.literal: Some discussants said law is hard to change often; this is the common man's incomprehension, doubting and blocking the way of governance-the very problem sages always worry about.
translations.idiomatic: The text rejects conservative legal inertia as a recurring obstacle to effective governance.

sentences.zh: 故略舉漢興以來，法令稍定而合古便今者。

translations.literal: Therefore, in brief, from Han's rise onward, laws and ordinances gradually stabilized where they fit antiquity and suited the present.
translations.idiomatic: It then summarizes instances where Han law moved toward both classical legitimacy and practical utility.

sentences.zh: 周官有五聽、八議、三刺、三宥、三赦之法。

translations.literal: Zhou offices had methods of Five Hearings, Eight Deliberations, Three Inquiries, Three Pardons, and Three Exemptions.
translations.idiomatic: Classical Zhou jurisprudence included multi-layered evidentiary and mitigation frameworks.

sentences.zh: 五聽：一曰辭聽，二曰色聽，三曰氣聽，四曰耳聽，五曰目聽。

translations.literal: Five Hearings: first speech-hearing, second color-hearing, third qi-hearing, fourth ear-hearing, fifth eye-hearing.
translations.idiomatic: The Five Hearings evaluated testimony through words, expression, breath, auditory clues, and visual observation.

sentences.zh: 八議：一曰議親，二曰議故，三曰議賢，四曰議能，五曰議功，六曰議貴，七曰議勤，八曰議賓。

translations.literal: Eight Deliberations: deliberation of kinship, old ties, worthiness, ability, merit, rank, diligence, and guest status.
translations.idiomatic: The Eight Deliberations were status-based mitigating categories covering kinship, merit, office, and diplomatic standing.

sentences.zh: 三刺：一曰訊群臣，二曰訊群吏，三曰訊萬民。

translations.literal: Three Inquiries: inquire of ministers, inquire of officials, inquire of the people.
translations.idiomatic: Judgment involved consultation across ministers, administrators, and populace.

sentences.zh: 三宥：一曰弗識，二曰過失，三曰遺忘。

translations.literal: Three Pardons: ignorance, error, forgetfulness.
translations.idiomatic: Three pardonable conditions were non-knowledge, mistake, and lapse of memory.

sentences.zh: 三赦：一曰幼弱，二曰老眊，三曰憃愚。

translations.literal: Three Exemptions: young weakness, aged senility, and foolish dullness.
translations.idiomatic: Legal exemption categories included very young, very old, and intellectually limited persons.

sentences.zh: 凡囚，「上罪梏拲而桎，中罪梏桎，下罪梏；

translations.literal: As for prisoners: 'Upper crimes cuffed and fettered; middle crimes cuffed and fettered; lower crimes cuffed;
translations.idiomatic: Custodial restraint was graded by offense severity.

sentences.zh: 王之同族拲，有爵者桎，以待弊。」

translations.literal: members of the king's own clan are only cuffed; those with rank only fettered, awaiting final outcome.'
translations.idiomatic: Royal kin and ranked elites received modified detention forms pending disposition.

sentences.zh: 高皇帝七年，制詔御史：「獄之疑者，吏或不敢決，有罪者久而不論，無罪者久繫不決。

translations.literal: In Gaozu year seven, an edict to Censors said: 'In doubtful cases officials sometimes dare not decide; the guilty remain long untried and innocents remain long detained undecided.'
translations.idiomatic: An early Han edict targeted delay and non-decision in doubtful criminal cases.

sentences.zh: 自今以來，縣道官獄疑者，各讞所屬二千石官，二千石官以其罪名當報之。

translations.literal: 'From now, doubtful county and district prison cases should each be reviewed by the relevant 2,000-shi official, and that official shall report applicable charge names.'
translations.idiomatic: Local doubtful cases were escalated to higher prefectural review.

sentences.zh: 所不能決者，皆移廷尉，廷尉亦當報之。

translations.literal: 'What they cannot decide all transfer to the Chief Justice, who should also report.'
translations.idiomatic: Unresolved matters moved upward to central judicial review.

sentences.zh: 廷尉所不能決，謹具為奏，傅所當比律令以聞。」

translations.literal: 'What the Chief Justice cannot decide, carefully memorialize and attach the matching statutes and ordinances for hearing.'
translations.idiomatic: Ultimate hard cases required full memorial with legal analogues attached.

sentences.zh: 上恩如此，吏猶不能奉宣。

translations.literal: Such was imperial grace, yet officials still could not properly promulgate and execute.
translations.idiomatic: Despite clear directives, administrative implementation remained weak.

sentences.zh: 故孝景中五年復下詔曰：「諸獄疑，雖文致於法而於人心不厭者，輒讞之。」

translations.literal: Therefore in Jingdi middle year five another edict said: 'In all doubtful prisons, though textually fitting statute but not satisfying human feeling, immediately refer them for review.'
translations.idiomatic: A later edict insisted that mechanical textual fit be checked against moral plausibility.

sentences.zh: 其後獄吏復避微文，遂其愚心。

translations.literal: Afterward prison officials again hid behind minor textualities, following their own foolish intent.
translations.idiomatic: Yet legal formalism reasserted itself through pedantic loopholing.

sentences.zh: 至後元年，又下詔曰：「獄，重事也。

translations.literal: In Houyuan year one another edict said: 'Prisons are weighty matters.
translations.idiomatic: Another imperial intervention stressed the gravity of criminal adjudication.

sentences.zh: 人有愚智，官有上下。

translations.literal: People have wisdom or stupidity; officials have high and low rank.
translations.idiomatic: Human and administrative capacities vary;

sentences.zh: 獄疑者讞，有令讞者已報讞而後不當，讞者不為失。」

translations.literal: Doubtful cases should be reviewed; when ordered review has already reported review but later proves improper, the reviewer is not counted at fault.'
translations.idiomatic: Reviewers were shielded from liability for good-faith error to encourage candid referral.

sentences.zh: 自此之後，獄刑益詳，近於五聽三宥之意。

translations.literal: After this, prison penalties became more detailed, approaching the intent of Five Hearings and Three Pardons.
translations.idiomatic: The system moved somewhat closer to classical evidentiary and mitigation principles.

sentences.zh: 三年復下詔曰：「高年老長，人所尊敬也；

translations.literal: Three years later another edict said: 'The very old are those people honor and respect;
translations.idiomatic: Subsequent policy emphasized humanitarian protections for vulnerable groups.

sentences.zh: 鰥寡不屬逮者，人所哀憐也。

translations.literal: widowers and widows not under summons are those people pity.
translations.idiomatic: Certain socially vulnerable persons were marked for special consideration.

sentences.zh: 其著令：年八十以上，八歲以下，及孕者未乳，師、朱儒當鞠繫者，頌繫之。」

translations.literal: Set this in statute: those over eighty, below eight, and pregnant women before weaning, as well as teachers and dwarfs who should be arrested, are to be held by summons rather than bound detention.'
translations.idiomatic: The edict introduced age, pregnancy, and bodily-status protections in custodial procedure.

sentences.zh: 至孝宣元康四年，又下詔曰：「

translations.literal: By Emperor Xuan Yuankang year four, another edict said:
translations.idiomatic: In Yuankang 4 Emperor Xuan issued another humanitarian correction.

sentences.zh: 朕念夫耆老之人，髮齒墮落，血氣既衰，亦無暴逆之心，今或羅于文法，執于囹圄，不得終其年命，朕甚憐之。

translations.literal: 'I think of old people whose hair and teeth have fallen, blood and qi declined, with no violent rebellious heart; now some are trapped in legal text, seized in prisons, unable to finish their lifespan-I deeply pity this.'
translations.idiomatic: He emphasized mercy for frail elders swept into penal machinery despite low social threat.

sentences.zh: 自今以來，諸年八十非誣告殺傷人，它皆勿坐。」

translations.literal: 'From now, all over eighty not falsely accusing, killing, or injuring others shall not be convicted for other matters.'
translations.idiomatic: The policy narrowed elder criminal liability to severe or malicious offenses.

sentences.zh: 至成帝鴻嘉元年，定令：「年未滿七歲，賊鬥殺人及犯殊死者，上請廷尉以聞，得減死。」

translations.literal: In Chengdi Hongjia year one, an order fixed: 'If not yet seven years old, and in robbery-fighting homicide or capital offenses, submit to Chief Justice for hearing and may receive commuted death.'
translations.idiomatic: Child offenders under seven in grave cases required central review and could receive death commutation.

sentences.zh: 合於三赦幼弱老眊之人。

translations.literal: This accords with the three-exemption principle for young and old-senile persons.
translations.idiomatic: The measure was framed as consistent with classical exemption doctrine.

sentences.zh: 此皆法令稍定，近古而便民者也。

translations.literal: These are all cases where statutes gradually stabilized, approached antiquity, and benefited the people.
translations.idiomatic: Such revisions are presented as successful examples of humane legal normalization.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「如有王者，必世而後仁；

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'If there were a true king, it would take a generation before benevolence;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius taught that deep moral governance takes generational time.

sentences.zh: 善人為國百年，可以勝殘去殺矣。」

translations.literal: a good man governing a state for a hundred years could overcome cruelty and remove killing.'
translations.idiomatic: Even non-sage but good governance, sustained long enough, can reduce violence.

sentences.zh: 言聖王承衰撥亂而起，被民以德教，變而化之，必世然後仁道成焉；

translations.literal: He meant that a sage king arising after decline and disorder, covering people with virtue and teaching, transforming them, requires a generation before benevolent order is completed;
translations.idiomatic: Moral transformation is gradual and depends on long-term virtue-centered statecraft.

sentences.zh: 至於善人，不入於室，然猶百年勝殘去殺矣。

translations.literal: as for a good man, though not entering the inner chamber, still after a hundred years can overcome cruelty and remove killing.
translations.idiomatic: Even second-best rulers, if persistent, can still greatly reduce penal violence over time.

sentences.zh: 此為國者之程式也。

translations.literal: This is the standard procedure for those governing states.
translations.idiomatic: This sets the proper timeline expectation for political reform.

sentences.zh: 今漢道至盛，歷世二百餘載，考自昭、宣、元、成、哀、平六世之間，斷獄殊死，率歲千餘口而一人，耐罪上至右止，三倍有餘。

translations.literal: Now Han's way reached flourishing for over two hundred years; examining the six reigns from Zhao, Xuan, Yuan, Cheng, Ai, Ping, capital judgments averaged over a thousand persons per year and one person in proportion, while punishments from exile up to right-foot penalty were over triple.
translations.idiomatic: Across the mid-late Western Han reigns, death sentencing remained high and severe non-capital punishment even more prevalent.

sentences.zh: 古人有言：「滿堂而飲酒，有一人鄉隅而悲泣，則一堂皆為之不樂。」

translations.literal: An old saying says: 'If a hall is drinking and one person in a corner weeps, then the whole hall is unhappy.'
translations.idiomatic: Classical moral analogies insist that one sufferer can indict collective order.

sentences.zh: 王者之於天下，譬猶一堂之上也，故一人不得其平，為之悽愴於心。

translations.literal: For a king toward all under heaven, it is like one hall; therefore if one person does not obtain fairness, the king is pained in heart.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler should treat each unjust case as a stain on the whole polity.

sentences.zh: 今郡國被刑而死者歲以萬數，天下獄二千餘所，其冤死者多少相覆，獄不減一人，此和氣所以未洽者也。

translations.literal: Now those executed by punishment in commanderies and kingdoms are in tens of thousands yearly; there are over two thousand prisons under heaven; how many unjust deaths overlap! If prisoners do not decrease by even one person, this is why harmonious qi has not fully spread.
translations.idiomatic: Mass incarceration and high execution counts are cited as causes of unresolved social disharmony.

sentences.zh: 原獄刑所以蕃若此者，禮教不立，刑法不明，民多貧窮，豪桀務私，姦不輒得，獄豻不平之所致也。

translations.literal: Looking to why prison penalties are so numerous: ritual teaching is not established, penal law not clear, people often poor, powerful men pursue private ends, crimes not promptly caught, and prison adjudication uneven-these cause it.
translations.idiomatic: The text attributes penal overgrowth to moral-institutional failure, socioeconomic stress, elite predation, and uneven enforcement.

sentences.zh: 《書》云「伯夷降典，悊民惟刑」，言制禮以止刑，猶隄之防溢水也。

translations.literal: The Documents says, 'Bo Yi descended with canons, wise people rely on punishment,' meaning rites are made to stop punishment, like dikes preventing flood overflow.
translations.idiomatic: Ritual is framed as preventive infrastructure; punishment is secondary flood-control.

sentences.zh: 今隄防凌遲，禮制未立；

translations.literal: Now dikes and defenses are decayed, and ritual institutions not yet established;
translations.idiomatic: With normative institutions eroded, legal pressure rises.

sentences.zh: 死刑過制，生刑易犯；

translations.literal: death penalties exceed proper measure, while lesser penalties are easy to incur;
translations.idiomatic: Capital punishment is overused while minor liability is overly broad;

sentences.zh: 饑寒並至，窮斯濫溢；

translations.literal: hunger and cold arrive together, and desperation then overflows;
translations.idiomatic: material deprivation pushes people into offense;

sentences.zh: 豪桀擅私，為之囊橐，姦有所隱，則狃而寖廣：此刑之所以蕃也。

translations.literal: powerful men wield private influence, becoming pouches and sacks for wrongdoing; when crime is hidden somewhere, habituation spreads wider-this is why punishment proliferates.
translations.idiomatic: elite shielding networks conceal crime, normalizing broader lawlessness and expanding punitive response.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「古之知法者能省刑，本也；

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'Those who understood law in antiquity could reduce punishments-this is root;
translations.idiomatic: Ancient legal wisdom aimed first at shrinking punishment, which is foundational;

sentences.zh: 今之知法者不失有罪，末矣。」

translations.literal: those who understand law now merely do not miss the guilty-this is branch.'
translations.idiomatic: modern practice prioritizes conviction accuracy over structural penal reduction, a secondary concern.

sentences.zh: 又曰：「今之聽獄者，求所以殺之；

translations.literal: He also said: 'Those who hear cases now seek how to kill;
translations.idiomatic: Current adjudicators seek grounds to condemn;

sentences.zh: 古之聽獄者，求所以生之。」

translations.literal: those who heard cases in antiquity sought how to let live.'
translations.idiomatic: ancient adjudicators sought grounds to preserve life.

sentences.zh: 與其殺不辜，寧失有罪。

translations.literal: Rather than kill the innocent, better to let the guilty slip.
translations.idiomatic: The preferred error is under-punishment over wrongful killing.

sentences.zh: 今之獄吏，上下相驅，以刻為明，深者獲功名，平者多後患。
