translations.literal: Now prison officials, upper and lower, drive one another; they take severity as clarity, the harsher gain merit and fame, the balanced incur many later troubles.
translations.idiomatic: Bureaucratic incentives reward harshness and punish moderation.

sentences.zh: 諺曰：「鬻棺者欲歲之疫。」

translations.literal: A proverb says: 'The coffin seller desires epidemics each year.'
translations.idiomatic: As the proverb says, some professions profit from social catastrophe.

sentences.zh: 非憎人欲殺之，利在於人死也。

translations.literal: Not because he hates people and wants to kill them, but because his profit lies in people's deaths.
translations.idiomatic: The motive is profit structure, not explicit malice.

sentences.zh: 今治獄吏欲陷害人，亦猶此矣。

translations.literal: Now judicial officials wanting to frame and harm people are just like this.
translations.idiomatic: The same perverse incentive logic applies to predatory legal officials.

sentences.zh: 凡此五疾，獄刑所以尤多者也。

translations.literal: All these five diseases are why prison punishments are especially many.
translations.idiomatic: These five systemic pathologies drive penal overexpansion.

sentences.zh: 自建武、永平，民亦新免兵革之禍，人有樂生之慮，與高、惠之間同，而政在抑彊扶弱，朝無威福之臣，邑無豪桀之俠。

translations.literal: Since Jianwu and Yongping, people had newly escaped war calamities and cherished life, as between Gaozu and Hui; governance suppressed the strong and supported the weak, court lacked ministers wielding private awe and favor, and localities lacked heroic magnates.
translations.idiomatic: In early Eastern Han recovery, social conditions resembled early Han: pro-weak governance and reduced private coercive power.

sentences.zh: 以口率計，斷獄少於成、哀之間什八，可謂清矣。

translations.literal: By per-capita calculation, adjudicated prison cases were less than two-tenths of the Cheng-Ai interval, and may be called clear.
translations.idiomatic: Per capita criminal adjudication fell dramatically versus late Western Han, indicating relative legal clarity.

sentences.zh: 然而未能稱意比隆於古者，以其疾未盡除，而刑本不正。

translations.literal: Yet it still could not satisfy intent or equal antiquity's grandeur, because these diseases were not fully removed and punishment roots were not corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Even improved conditions fell short while structural causes and penal foundations remained uncorrected.

sentences.zh: 善乎！

translations.literal: Excellent!
translations.idiomatic: Well said!

sentences.zh: 孫卿之論刑也，曰：「世俗之為說者，以為治古者無肉刑，有象刑墨黥之屬，菲履赭衣而不純，是不然矣。

translations.literal: Xunzi's discussion of punishment says: 'Common talk says ancient good governance had no corporal penalties, only symbolic penalties like tattooing types and rough shoes/red clothes; this is incorrect.
translations.idiomatic: Xunzi rejects the myth that ideal antiquity relied only on symbolic penalties and no corporal sanctions.

sentences.zh: 以為治古，則人莫觸罪邪，豈獨無肉刑哉，亦不待象刑矣。

translations.literal: If one says in well-governed antiquity people did not commit crimes, then not only no corporal penalties, no symbolic penalties were needed either.
translations.idiomatic: If crime truly vanished, all penalties-not just corporal ones-would be unnecessary.

sentences.zh: 以為人或觸罪矣，而直輕其刑，是殺人者不死，而傷人者不刑也。

translations.literal: If one says people still committed crimes but penalties were merely light, then killers would not die and injurers not be punished.
translations.idiomatic: Simply lightening punishment without moral order would nullify deterrence for grave violence.

sentences.zh: 罪至重而刑至輕，民無所畏，亂莫大焉。

translations.literal: When crime reaches heavy but punishment reaches light, people fear nothing; no disorder is greater.
translations.idiomatic: Severe crime with weak sanction creates maximal disorder.

sentences.zh: 凡制刑之本，將以禁暴惡，且懲其

translations.literal: The root of setting punishment is to prohibit violence and evil, and to chastise their
translations.idiomatic: Punishment's core function is to stop violent harm and constrain its

sentences.zh: 末也。

translations.literal: extremes.
translations.idiomatic: extreme expressions.

sentences.zh: 殺人者不死，傷人者不刑，是惠暴而寬惡也。

translations.literal: If killers do not die and injurers are not punished, this is benefiting violence and being lenient to evil.
translations.idiomatic: Excessive leniency toward violent offenders is itself injustice to social order.

sentences.zh: 故象刑非生治古，方起於亂今也。

translations.literal: Therefore symbolic punishment did not arise in flourishing antiquity; it emerged from disordered present times.
translations.idiomatic: So-called symbolic punishment is not a pristine ancient ideal but a product of later disorder.

sentences.zh: 凡爵列官職，賞慶刑罰，皆以類相從者也。

translations.literal: In general, rank, office, rewards, celebrations, and punishments all follow corresponding categories.
translations.idiomatic: Political order requires proportional correspondence among status, reward, and penalty.

sentences.zh: 一物失稱，亂之端也。

translations.literal: If one item loses correspondence, it is the beginning of disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Any mismatch in these correspondences opens a path to institutional chaos.

sentences.zh: 德不稱位，能不稱官，賞不當功，刑不當罪，不祥莫大矣焉。

translations.literal: Virtue not matching position, ability not matching office, reward not fitting merit, punishment not fitting crime-no inauspiciousness is greater.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing is more ruinous than systemic misalignment between merit, office, reward, and sanction.

sentences.zh: 夫征暴誅悖，治之威也。

translations.literal: To campaign against violence and execute rebellion is governance's authority.
translations.idiomatic: Coercive suppression of rebellion is an essential dimension of state authority.

sentences.zh: 殺人者死，傷人者刑，是百王之所同也，未有知其所由來者也。

translations.literal: Killers die, injurers are punished-this is shared by all hundred kings; none know its beginning.
translations.idiomatic: Across dynasties this principle remained constant and foundational.

sentences.zh: 故治則刑重，亂則刑輕，犯治之罪固重，犯亂之罪固輕也。

translations.literal: Therefore in well-governed times punishment is heavy, in chaotic times punishment is light; offending order's crimes are inherently heavy, offending chaos's crimes inherently light.
translations.idiomatic: Xunzi paradoxically argues that robust order permits stricter sanction because normative standards are higher.

sentences.zh: 《書》云『刑罰世重世輕』，此之謂也。」

translations.literal: The Documents says 'penalties are heavy in one age and light in another'-this is what it means.
translations.idiomatic: This is the meaning of the classical phrase about historically variable penal weight.

sentences.zh: 所謂「象刑惟明」者，言象天道而作刑，安有菲屨赭衣者哉？

translations.literal: What is called 'symbolic punishment only clarity' means modeling Heaven's way in making punishment-how could there be rough shoes and red clothes only?
translations.idiomatic: 'Symbolic punishment' should be read cosmologically, not as mere costume penalties.

sentences.zh: 孫卿之言既然，又因俗說而論之曰：禹承堯舜之後，自以德衰而制肉刑，湯武順而行之者，以俗薄於唐虞故也。

translations.literal: Xunzi having said this, further arguing from common opinion: Yu inherited after Yao-Shun and because his virtue had declined made corporal punishments; Tang and Wu followed because customs were thinner than Tang-Yu.
translations.idiomatic: He reinterprets historical shifts as responses to declining social-moral conditions.

sentences.zh: 今漢承衰周暴秦極敝之流，俗已薄於三代，而行堯舜之刑，是猶以鞿而御駻突，違救時之宜矣。

translations.literal: Now Han inherits the exhausted flow of declining Zhou and violent Qin; customs are already thinner than Three Dynasties, yet practicing Yao-Shun punishments is like controlling a fierce horse with light tack-contrary to timely rescue.
translations.idiomatic: Given post-Zhou/Qin social deterioration, applying ultra-lenient antiquarian models is seen as impractical and dangerous.

sentences.zh: 且除肉刑者，本欲以全民也，今去髡鉗一等，轉而入於大辟。

translations.literal: Moreover, abolishing corporal punishment originally intended preserving life; now removing one level of shaving-shackling turns into entry into capital punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Commutation policy can perversely increase death sentencing if replacement scales are misdesigned.

sentences.zh: 以死罔民，失本惠矣。

translations.literal: To net people with death is to lose the original benevolent intent.
translations.idiomatic: That outcome betrays the reform's humanitarian purpose.

sentences.zh: 故死者歲以萬數，刑重之所致也。

translations.literal: Therefore yearly deaths in tens of thousands are caused by over-heavy punishment.
translations.idiomatic: High annual execution counts are attributed to excessive penal severity.

sentences.zh: 至乎穿窬之盜，忿怒傷人，男女淫佚，吏為姦臧，若此之惡，髡鉗之罰又不足以懲也。

translations.literal: As for burglars tunneling walls, rage-injury assaults, sexual licentiousness, and officials committing corruption-these evils are not sufficiently deterred by shaving-shackling penalties.
translations.idiomatic: Conversely, many offenses are under-deterred by too-light sanctions.

sentences.zh: 故刑者歲十萬數，民既不畏，又曾不恥，刑輕之所生也。

translations.literal: Therefore yearly punishments number in hundred-thousands; people neither fear nor feel shame-this is born of overly light punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Mass punishment without moral effect indicates miscalibration at the lenient end too.

sentences.zh: 故俗之能吏，公以殺盜為威，專殺者勝任，奉法者不治，亂名傷制，不可勝條。

translations.literal: Thus capable officials by custom make killing bandits their prestige; those specializing in killing are deemed competent, those following law are called incapable; reputations and institutions are damaged beyond listing.
translations.idiomatic: Administrative culture came to reward lethal zeal over lawful balance, corroding institutional norms.

sentences.zh: 是以罔密而姦不塞，刑蕃而民愈嫚。

translations.literal: Therefore nets grow denser but crime not blocked; punishments proliferate and people grow more insolent.
translations.idiomatic: Legal expansion without legitimacy produces both more law and more defiance.

sentences.zh: 必世而未仁，百年而不勝殘，誠以禮樂闕而刑不正也。

translations.literal: A generation passes without benevolence, a hundred years without overcoming cruelty-truly because rites and music are lacking and punishment is not correct.
translations.idiomatic: Failure over long horizons is traced to absent ritual order and distorted penal structure.

sentences.zh: 豈宜惟思所以清原正本之論，刪定律令，篹二百章，以應大辟。

translations.literal: Should one not only think on arguments for clearing source and correcting root, revising statutes and ordinances, compiling two hundred chapters matching capital law?
translations.idiomatic: The text calls for foundational legal redrafting into a coherent, compressed code.

sentences.zh: 其餘罪次，於古當生，今觸死者，皆可募行肉刑。

translations.literal: As for remaining offenses, those that in antiquity should have lived but now touch death may all be allowed to substitute corporal punishment.
translations.idiomatic: It proposes commuting many currently capital crimes to calibrated corporal sanctions.

sentences.zh: 及傷人與盜，吏受賕枉法，男女淫亂，皆復古刑，為三千章。

translations.literal: As for injuring others and theft, officials taking bribes and twisting the law, and sexual disorder between men and women, all should return to ancient punishments, forming three thousand articles.
translations.idiomatic: For assault, theft, corrupt officials who pervert the law, and sexual misconduct, penalties should be reset to the older classical scale, yielding a code of three thousand provisions.

sentences.zh: 詆欺文致微細之法，悉蠲除。

translations.literal: All laws of slanderous trickery and hair-splitting textual framing should be completely removed.
translations.idiomatic: All petty, manipulative, and overly technical legal provisions should be abolished entirely.

sentences.zh: 如此，則刑可畏而禁易避，吏不專殺，法無二門，輕重當罪，民命得全，合刑罰之中，殷天人之和，順稽古之制，成時雍之化。

translations.literal: If so, punishments will be fearsome yet prohibitions easy to avoid; officials will not monopolize killing; law will have no second gate; light and heavy will match crimes; people's lives can be preserved; this accords with proper balance in punishments, enriches harmony between Heaven and humans, follows and examines ancient institutions, and completes a transformation of timely concord.
translations.idiomatic: If this is done, the law will be stern yet navigable, officials will no longer wield death arbitrarily, punishments will fit offenses, and lives will be preserved. It would restore balanced penal governance, deepen harmony between Heaven and human society, align with tested ancient institutions, and produce an age of social concord.

sentences.zh: 成康刑錯，雖未可致，孝文斷獄，庶幾可及。

translations.literal: Though we may not yet attain the age of Cheng and Kang when punishments were set aside, we may perhaps approach Emperor Wen's judgments in criminal cases.
translations.idiomatic: We may not reach the ideal era of Cheng and Kang, when punishment was scarcely needed, but we can still aspire to the humane judicial standard associated with Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 《詩》云「宜民宜人，受祿于天」。

translations.literal: The Odes says: 'Making the people fitting, making persons fitting, one receives Heaven's blessing.'
translations.idiomatic: As the Odes says, 'When governance truly suits the people, Heaven grants its favor.'

sentences.zh: 《書》曰「立功立事，可以永年」。

translations.literal: The Documents says: 'Establish achievement and establish institutions, and one can endure for long years.'
translations.idiomatic: The Documents says that when real achievements and institutions are established, the state can endure over the long term.

sentences.zh: 言為政而宜於民者，功成事立，則受天祿而永年命，所謂「一人有慶，萬民賴之」者也。

translations.literal: This means that if one governs in a way suitable to the people, achievements are completed and institutions established; then one receives Heaven's blessing and long life, which is what is called 'when one person has good fortune, the myriad people rely on it.'
translations.idiomatic: In other words, governance that truly benefits the people creates lasting achievement and institutions. Such rule receives Heaven's favor and endures, fulfilling the saying: 'When one person is blessed, all the people depend on it.'
